---
title: "Assignment 7: Lightning simulation"
author: "Boglarka Ludmany"
output: html_document
date: "r format(Sys.time(), '%B, %Y')"
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
  ##rmd metadata: look into it
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
library(tidyverse)
```


# Task

- Create a random lightning algorithm, and visualize the result!
- The lightning should be a random walk from top to bottom.
- At each step, there is a 1% chance of forking.
- Each fork in itself is a new random walk.
- The forks do not need to fork further.
- Visualize the lightning, preferably using black background and blue/white foreground color. 
- (extra point) Try to do this using no loops! (loops are ok, but you can earn less points)
- (extra point) Create an animation using gganimate, where the lightning hits from above to below.

# Dataset

There is no dataset for this project. You have to simulate from scratch!

# Example lightning

![Lightning](assignment_7_example/example_lightning.png)

## Loading libraries
```{r}
library(purrr)
library(magrittr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
```


## Creating the function, that makes the random walk possible 
This random walk function creates the main branch of the random walk. Here, the random walk should have a strong direction downwards (y dimension), so that it is biased in y direction. This is defined by the mu_y parameter, which is the drift parameter for y (the expexted displacement per step). Here, it determines the vertical drift, which is supposed to be downwards (the lightning moves downwards).
In the x dimension, the random walk should have small side-by-side movements, but here, since mu_x is close to 0, and not used, the random walk has no preferred direction, the movement is rather random. 
The random walk starts from the x0 and y0 points, and each following point is determined by the mu_x, mu_y, and the z_x and z_y parameters. The number of steps in the random walk is represented by N. Each step is an independent draw from a normal distribution, and then the cumulative sum of these draws is created by `cumsum()`. In the z_y and z_x parameters, with the `cumsum()` function, the steps are added together, creating the position for each step, so that in each point in time, the sum of the steps define "where" that point is. 
The variance parameters determine the variance of each step. `variance_x` determines the horizontal "zig-zag" of the random walk, and `variance_y` determines the amount of noise in the vertical movement of the random walk. `variance_x` should be greater than `variance_y`, because the vertical movement should be stable (downwards), but the horizontal movement can have noise (even if a small amount). 


the parameters of the Gaussian random walk: 
- mean (μ) which determines in which direction (left/right) the random walk is actually going: 
  - if μ = 0 - there is no preferred direction
  - if μ != 0 - there is a preferred direction
- variance (σ): determines the expected displacement per step
  - if σ is small -> the line is smoother, straighter
  - if σ is bigger -> the line is cikázik
here: the mean should be close to 0 on the x axis (no preferred direction), and bigger than 0 on the y axis (the y axis direction is downwards) 

```{r}
RW <- function(N, x0, mu_x, variance_x, y0, mu_y, variance_y) {
  z_x <- cumsum(rnorm(n = N, mean = 0, sd = sqrt(variance_x))) ## the mean is 0: there is no preferred direction, the randomness is only defined by the variance_x 
  z_y <- cumsum(rnorm(n = N, mean = mu_y, sd = sqrt(variance_y))) 
  x <- x0 + z_x 
  y <- y0 + z_y
  data.frame(x = x, y = y) ## here, I specify that the preferred data type that this function should return is a data frame
}
```

In order to make sure that the function worked correctly, I visualized a random walk with the `plot()` function. 
```{r}
P1 <- RW(N = 100, x0 = 0, mu_x = 0, variance_x = 0.001, y0 = 0, mu_y = 0.05, variance_y = 0.0005)

main_branch_test <- plot(P1, type = "l", col = "red")
```

## Creating the main branch (with the RW() function)
The parameters of the main_branch: 
x0: starting position in the x dimension. Is 0, because: 
mu_x: is 0, because: 
variance_x: determines the expected displacement on the x axis. 
y0: starting point in the y dimension. Is 0, because: 
mu_y: is greater than mu_x because: .
variance_y: is greater than variance_x, since the vertical movement is more stable than the horizontal movement (as mentioned before). 
```{r}
N_main <- 1500 ## number of steps in the main branch - N should be great enough

main_branch <- RW(N = N_main, x0 = 0, mu_x = 0, variance_x = 0.00001, y0 = 0, mu_y = 0.035, variance_y = 0.00005) 
```

# ======================================================
## Creating the fork points 
```{r}
## from here, code is not commented - and the markdown is still a work in progress
main_branch_forks <- main_branch %>% 
  mutate(random = sample(1:N_main, n(), replace = T), ## generate random number between 1 and 1000 for every step
         fork = random < 10, 
         t = row_number()) ## flag where random < 10 -> probability that random < 10 is 1% -> the step is a fork point 
```

```{r}
## filtereltem hogy legyen egy df-em csak a fork pointokkal, majd ebbol csinaltam egy listat, hogy az lapply-t tudjam hasznalni a fork pointok random walkjanak elkeszitesehez 
fork_points <- main_branch_forks %>% 
  filter(fork != FALSE)
fork_list <- split(fork_points, seq(nrow(fork_points)))

## minden egyes fork pointhoz elkeszitettem a random walkot -> list of 5 fork points, mindegyikhez van egy data frame hozzarendelve a sajat random walkjaval

forkpoint_rw <- lapply(
  fork_list, 
  function(row) {
    N_fork <- N_main - row$t ## a main branch hatralevo lepeseinek a szama -> igy egy vonalban "fejezodik be" az osszes branch
    if (N_fork < 10) return(NULL)
    
    direction <- sample(c(-1, 1), 1) * 0.00008 ## itt a sample veletlenszeruen kikop egy erteket a {-1, 1} halmazbol, es ezt megszorozzuk 0.00002vel, tehat a direction vagy -0.000002 vagy 0.000002 --> tehat a mu nem pontosan 0 lesz, hanem egy picit vagy kisebb, vagy nagyobb mint 0 -> a fork agak "kulonvalnak a main branchtol"
    RW(N = N_fork, 
       x0 = row$x, mu_x = direction, 
       variance_x = 0.00003, ## nagyobb mint a main branche -> ne legyen cikazas  
       y0 = row$y, mu_y = 0.035, variance_y = 0.000005)
      } 
)
```

```{r}
## osszefuzni mindezt -> majd ebbol elkesziteni a vizualizaciot 
fork_rw_df <- bind_rows(forkpoint_rw, .id = "branch")

main_branch <- main_branch %>% 
  mutate(branch = "0") ## itt ezt azert csinalom, hogy a branch id-ja 0 legyen a main branchnak -> igy meg lehessen kulonboztetni hogy melyik a main branch es melyik a fork branch

final_rw <- bind_rows(main_branch, fork_rw_df)

final_rw <- final_rw %>% 
  group_by(branch) %>%
  mutate(t = row_number() + ## itt ezzel az ifelse-vel csak azt hatarozom meg, hogy melyik branchen hogy szamitsa ki a t-t -> a branch indexek alapjan
           if_else(branch == "0", 0, ## itt ha a branch 0, akkor az a main branch -> a main branch t-je csak folytatodik, nem csinal vele semmit 
                   first(fork_points$t[as.integer(branch)]))) %>% ## ha a branch t-je nem 0, akkor: (a branch tipusa integer!), csak onnan folytatja a szamolast, ahol a branch id nem 0, tehat megszuletett egy fork ag - es a first azert kell, mert a fork_points$t[branch] vektornak az elso erteket kell felvennie - csak egy biztositek az errorok ellen, ha esetleg ez a vektor tobb erteket dobna ki 
  ungroup()
```

```{r}
library(gganimate)
library(gifski)
lightning_plot <- ggplot(final_rw, aes(x = x, y = y, group = branch)) +
  geom_path(
    color = "white", 
    linewidth = 0.5 
  ) +
  scale_y_reverse() +
  theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "black", color = NA), 
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "black", color = NA)
  ) +
  transition_reveal(t)

animate(lightning_plot, renderer = gifski_renderer()) ## a renderer gifski, mert a magick rendererrel valamiert nem futott le az animacio

# anim_save("lightning.gif", animation = last_animation())
```
